nodes.name: ch16_necking_loss_001
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Disappearance of necking deformation in bcc metals at low temperature with high fluence, leading to totally brittle fracture

condition.value: bcc metals, low temperature, high fluence

prop_source.text_excerpt: In bcc metals tested at low temperature, high fluences may even result in the disappearance of necking deformation with fracture occurring on the elastic line

nodes.name: irradiation_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Increase in yield strength due to radiation-induced defects including defect clusters, impurity-defect complexes, dislocation loops, dislocation lines, voids, bubbles, and precipitates. Most pronounced at irradiation temperatures below 0.3Tm.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Irradiation-induced hardening in both fcc and bcc metals is caused by the production of the various defects

nodes.name: ductility_reduction_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Reduction in ductility (measured by total elongation or uniform elongation) due to irradiation. The approach of yield strength to ultimate tensile strength results in loss of ductility. At limit where yield strength equals ultimate tensile strength, uniform elongation is zero.

prop_source.text_excerpt: the ductility (measured either by total elongation or by uniform elongation) is reduced

nodes.name: source_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Increase in stress required to start a dislocation moving on its glide plane due to irradiation-produced defect clusters in the vicinity of Frank-Read sources. The stress required to release a dislocation is called the unpinning or unlocking stress.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Source hardening is the increase in stress required to start a dislocation moving on its glide plane

nodes.name: friction_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Stress required to sustain plastic deformation, often termed the flow stress or friction stress. Caused by long-range and short-range obstacles including dislocation network, defect clusters, loops, precipitates, and voids that impede dislocation motion.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Friction hardening refers to the stress required to sustain plastic deformation

nodes.name: total_brittleness_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: In bcc metals tested at low temperature with high fluences, necking deformation may disappear and fracture occurs on the elastic line, rendering them totally brittle.

prop_source.text_excerpt: high fluences may even result in the disappearance of necking deformation with fracture occurring on the elastic line

nodes.name: bcc_upper_lower_yield_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_label: BCC Upper and Lower Yield Points
nodes.prop_description: Manifestation of source hardening in bcc metals showing upper and lower yield points in stress-strain curves, caused by pinning or locking of dislocation lines by impurity atoms.

prop_source.text_excerpt: In unirradiated bcc metals, source hardening is manifest by the upper and lower yield points

nodes.name: fcc_gradual_yielding_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Gradual onset of yielding characteristic in fcc metals, explained by distribution of stresses required to operate Frank-Read sources. At low applied stress, dislocation sources with large separation between pinning points begin to generate dislocations.

prop_source.text_excerpt: The gradual onset of yielding characteristic in fcc metals is generally explained by a distribution of stresses

nodes.name: loop_unlocking_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Hardening mechanism where dislocation line segments unlock from small dislocation loops or defect clusters at stresses lower than required to bow and operate a Frank-Read source. For edge character loops with Burgers vector bl, radius r, and spacing l, the shear stress is approximately 0.09μb/l.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Singh suggests that y = 1.5r is consistent with the observed microstructure

nodes.name: dipole_network_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Yield stress estimated by stress necessary to overcome interaction between dislocation dipoles when loops form a network. Shear stress is approximately 0.06μb/y where y is the distance between dipoles.

prop_source.text_excerpt: unlocking process occurred by interaction between an edge segment and a network of loops

nodes.name: long_range_stress_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Forces arising from repulsive interaction between a moving dislocation and components of the dislocation network. Stress depends on dislocation density ρd as σLR = αμb√ρd where α ≈ 0.44.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Long-range forces arise from the repulsive interaction between a moving dislocation

nodes.name: short_range_stress_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Forces due to interaction between moving dislocation and discrete obstacles in the slip plane. Includes athermal (dislocation bowing) and thermally activated (cutting or climbing) processes. Total friction stress σF = σLR + σSR where σSR = σppt + σvoid + σloops.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Short-range forces are due to the interaction between a moving dislocation and an obstacle

nodes.name: hall_petch_hardening_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Yield strength dependence on grain size given by σy = σi + kyd^(-1/2) where σi is friction stress, ky is Hall-Petch coefficient, and d is grain diameter. Yield strength increases with decreasing grain size for nominal grain sizes (few to hundreds of micrometers).

trend.value: increasing strength with decreasing grain size

prop_source.text_excerpt: Equation (12.63) is the Hall–Petch equation, which describes the grain size dependence of the yield stress

nodes.name: yield_strength_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Stress at which plasticity begins, a key parameter in determining mechanical behavior of metals. In irradiated materials, yield strength increases much more than ultimate tensile strength, leading to ductility reduction.

prop_source.text_excerpt: The stress at which plasticity begins is called the yield stress, σy or YS

nodes.name: ultimate_tensile_strength_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Maximum load divided by original cross-sectional area of the sample. Irradiation increases yield strength much more than ultimate tensile strength, causing the approach of σy to σUTS and resulting in loss of ductility.

prop_source.text_excerpt: The tensile strength or ultimate tensile strength (UTS) is the maximum load divided by the original cross sectional area

nodes.name: uniform_strain_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Strain that occurs uniformly in the gage section of the sample up to the ultimate tensile strength when necking begins. True uniform strain εu is equal to strain hardening exponent n (εu = n) in power-law hardening.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Strain occurs uniformly in the gage section of the sample up to the UTS

nodes.name: true_uniform_strain_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Natural logarithm of the ratio of original area to area at maximum load: εu = ln(A0/Au). In power-law hardening, true uniform strain equals the strain hardening exponent n.

prop_source.text_excerpt: The true uniform strain, εu is given by the strain at maximum load

nodes.name: fracture_strain_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: True strain based on original area and area after fracture: εf = ln(A0/Af) = ln(1/(1-RA)) where RA is reduction in area at fracture.

prop_source.text_excerpt: The true fracture strain, εf is the true strain based on the original area and the area after fracture

nodes.name: local_necking_strain_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Strain required to deform the specimen from maximum load to fracture: εn = ln(Au/Af). Necking or localized deformation begins at the point of maximum load defined by dP = 0.

prop_source.text_excerpt: the local necking strain, εn is the strain required to deform the specimen from the maximum load to fracture

nodes.name: strain_hardening_exponent_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Exponent n in the power-law hardening relationship σ = Kε^n, where σ is stress, εp is plastic strain, and K is stress at εp = 1.0. Parameter n is between 0.1 and 0.5 typically, with n = 0 for perfectly plastic and n = 1 for elastic behavior.

prop_source.text_excerpt: n is the strain hardening exponent. Note that n = 0 for perfectly plastic behavior and n = 1 for elastic behavior

nodes.name: shear_modulus_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Material property relating shear stress to shear strain in elastic deformation. Appears in expressions for long-range stress hardening (σLR = αμb√ρd) and source hardening (σFR = μb/l).

prop_source.text_excerpt: where μ is the shear modulus

nodes.name: poissons_ratio_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Ratio of transverse strain to longitudinal strain in elastic deformation. Value is 0.25 for perfectly isotropic elastic materials, approximately 0.33 for most metals. Used in stress-strain relations and long-range stress calculations.

prop_source.text_excerpt: where ν is Poisson's ratio and has a value of 0.25 for perfectly isotropic elastic materials

nodes.name: bulk_modulus_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Ratio of hydrostatic pressure to dilatation, K = E/(3(1-2ν)). Represents volumetric modulus of elasticity. The hydrostatic component of stress produces only elastic volume changes and does not cause plastic deformation.

role.value: ratio of hydrostatic pressure to dilatation

prop_source.text_excerpt: K = E/(3(1-2ν)) is the bulk modulus or volumetric modulus of elasticity

nodes.name: youngs_modulus_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Modulus of elasticity in tension or compression, E. In elastic region, deformation is proportional to load according to Hooke's law: σ = Eε where σ is stress and ε is strain.

prop_source.text_excerpt: σ = Eε , where σ is stress, ε is strain and E is the modulus of elasticity in tension or compression

nodes.name: dislocation_density_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Number density of dislocations in material, ρd, affects long-range stress hardening. The distance between dislocations r is given by r = 1/√(πρd). Long-range stress σLR = αμb√ρd where α ≈ 0.44.

prop_source.text_excerpt: where ρd is the dislocation density gives: FLR = μb²√πρd/π^(4/3)

nodes.name: von_mises_yield_criterion_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Yielding occurs when second invariant of stress deviator exceeds critical value: σy = (1/√2)[(σ1-σ2)² + (σ2-σ3)² + (σ3-σ1)²]^(1/2). For pure shear, yield stress is k = σy/√3 = 0.577σy.

prop_source.text_excerpt: von Mises proposed that yielding will occur when the second invariant of the stress deviator

nodes.name: tresca_yield_criterion_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Maximum shear stress criterion: yielding occurs when maximum shear stress reaches shear stress in uniaxial tension test. For pure shear, yield stress is k = σy/2. Observed to hold fairly well in alloys exhibiting a yield drop.

prop_source.text_excerpt: Tresca (or maximum shear stress) criterion, which says that yielding occurs when the maximum shear stress

nodes.name: offset_yield_strength_ch17_p1
nodes.label: {name}
nodes.prop_description: Commonly accepted definition of yield stress determined by stress corresponding to intersection of stress-strain curve and line parallel to elastic portion offset by strain of 0.2%.

prop_source.text_excerpt: the offset yield strength, determined by the stress corresponding to the intersection of the stress-strain curve
